questions.q: In which year were the Pokhran-II nuclear tests conducted?
questions.explanation: India carried out the Pokhran-II series of nuclear tests in May 1998.

questions.q: What was the official code name for the Pokhran-II nuclear tests?
questions.explanation: The 1998 tests were carried out under the code name Operation Shakti, meaning 'power' or 'strength'.

questions.q: In which Indian state is the Pokhran test range located?
questions.explanation: The Pokhran test range lies in the Thar Desert region of Rajasthan.

questions.q: Who was the Prime Minister of India when Pokhran-II was conducted?
questions.explanation: Atal Bihari Vajpayee was Prime Minister when India conducted the Pokhran-II tests in 1998.

questions.q: What did the Pokhran-II tests establish India as?
questions.explanation: The 1998 tests established India as a declared nuclear-weapons state.

questions.q: The 1974 test at Pokhran, which preceded Pokhran-II, was code-named what?
questions.explanation: India's first nuclear test in 1974 was code-named Smiling Buddha; Pokhran-II in 1998 followed it.

questions.q: Pokhran-II is most directly associated with which type of weapon technology?
questions.explanation: Pokhran-II was a series of underground nuclear weapon tests.

questions.q: In which month of 1998 did the Pokhran-II tests take place?
questions.explanation: The tests were conducted on 11 and 13 May 1998.

questions.q: Which neighbouring country responded to Pokhran-II by conducting its own nuclear tests weeks later in 1998?
questions.explanation: Pakistan conducted its Chagai nuclear tests in late May 1998, weeks after Pokhran-II.

questions.q: What was the primary purpose of conducting the tests underground at Pokhran?
options: To grow crops in the desert
To contain the blast and reduce fallout
questions.explanation: Underground testing helps contain the explosion and limit the release of radioactive fallout.

questions.q: On which date were the first three Pokhran-II devices detonated?
questions.explanation: Three devices were detonated on 11 May 1998, with two more on 13 May 1998.

questions.q: How many nuclear devices did India announce it detonated in total during Pokhran-II?
questions.explanation: India announced a total of five detonations: three on 11 May and two on 13 May 1998.

questions.q: Which two organisations jointly led the development and execution of Pokhran-II?
questions.explanation: The DRDO and the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC) jointly carried out Pokhran-II.

questions.q: Which scientist, later President of India, was a key figure coordinating Pokhran-II?
questions.explanation: A. P. J. Abdul Kalam, then head of the DRDO, was a leading coordinator of Pokhran-II and later became President.

questions.q: Which nuclear scientist headed BARC and led the Atomic Energy Commission during Pokhran-II?
questions.explanation: R. Chidambaram, then Chairman of the Atomic Energy Commission, led the scientific effort at BARC for Pokhran-II.

questions.q: According to India, one of the 11 May 1998 devices was which advanced type of weapon?
questions.explanation: India claimed one device in the 11 May test was a thermonuclear (two-stage hydrogen bomb) device.

questions.q: What major international event did Pokhran-II prompt against India in 1998?
options: Economic sanctions led by the United States
questions.explanation: The United States and several other countries imposed economic sanctions on India following the 1998 tests.

questions.q: Pokhran-II is often cited as a reason India has not signed which arms-control treaty?
questions.explanation: India has not signed the CTBT, and the Pokhran-II tests reinforced its position on retaining the testing option.

questions.q: What policy did India formally articulate after becoming a declared nuclear power following Pokhran-II?
questions.explanation: India adopted a declared 'No First Use' nuclear doctrine after the 1998 tests.

questions.q: The day of the 11 May 1998 tests is commemorated annually in India as which day?
questions.explanation: 11 May is marked as National Technology Day in India, partly to commemorate the Pokhran-II tests.

questions.q: Which Indian Army facility/range is associated with the Pokhran nuclear test site?
questions.explanation: The tests were conducted at the Pokhran Test Range, an Indian Army field firing range in Rajasthan.

questions.q: A notable feature of Pokhran-II preparation was that it was carried out so as to evade detection by what?
questions.explanation: Elaborate secrecy measures were used to avoid detection by foreign reconnaissance satellites before the tests.

questions.q: The two devices tested on 13 May 1998 were primarily described as which yield class?
questions.explanation: The 13 May 1998 detonations were reported as sub-kiloton, low-yield devices.

questions.q: The shaft used for the largest 11 May 1998 thermonuclear device was given which code name?
questions.explanation: The shafts were given code names such as 'White House', 'Taj Mahal' and 'Kumbhkaran'; the thermonuclear device was placed in the 'White House' shaft.

questions.q: What yield did Indian scientists officially claim for the thermonuclear device tested on 11 May 1998?
questions.explanation: Indian officials claimed the two-stage thermonuclear device tested on 11 May 1998 yielded about 45 kilotons (roughly 43-45 kt).

questions.q: Who served as the DRDO field coordinator for the Pokhran-II tests?
options: K. Santhanam, the DRDO test coordinator
questions.explanation: K. Santhanam was the DRDO field test coordinator for Pokhran-II and later publicly debated the thermonuclear device's yield.

questions.q: What did K. Santhanam controversially claim years later about the 11 May 1998 thermonuclear test?
options: That it was a complete success beyond claims
That its yield fell short and it was a partial 'fizzle'
That it was never actually conducted
questions.explanation: In 2009 K. Santhanam claimed the thermonuclear device underperformed (a partial fizzle), sparking debate, though BARC officials disputed this.

questions.q: To preserve secrecy at the test site, the scientists and officials in the field disguised themselves as what?
questions.explanation: Scientists wore army uniforms and used military cover so satellite imagery would show only routine soldiers at the range.

questions.q: Which fissile material was central to India's weapons programme demonstrated through the Pokhran tests?
questions.explanation: Weapons-grade plutonium, produced in Indian reactors, was central to the fission and boosted devices tested at Pokhran.

questions.q: Pokhran-II ultimately contributed to a major 2008 diplomatic outcome with the United States. What was it?
options: India's entry into the UN Security Council
The India-U.S. Civil Nuclear Agreement (123 Agreement)
questions.explanation: Despite initial sanctions, the changed strategic landscape after 1998 eventually led to the 2008 India-U.S. Civil Nuclear (123) Agreement.
